title: WhatsApp / Chat — System Design
description: Design a real-time messaging system: deliver 1:1 and group messages instantly to online users, buffer them for offline users, and sync history across devices. The themes are persistent connections, delivery state machines, presence, and group fanout.

nodes.label: Mobile / Web Client
nodes.description: User's app; holds a persistent connection and a local message store.

nodes.label: Connection Load Balancer
nodes.description: Routes new connections to gateway nodes; sticky per connection.

nodes.label: WebSocket Gateway
nodes.description: Holds millions of long-lived connections; one user's devices map here while online.

nodes.label: Session / Presence Registry
nodes.description: Maps userId/deviceId -> which gateway holds the live connection; tracks online status.

nodes.label: Chat Service
nodes.description: Validates, persists, and routes messages; owns delivery semantics.

nodes.label: Per-User Message Queue
nodes.description: Inbox of undelivered messages per recipient (offline buffer).

nodes.label: Message Store
nodes.description: Durable messages, sharded by conversation/user for history sync.

nodes.label: Group Fanout Service
nodes.description: Expands a group message to each member's queue/gateway.

nodes.label: Group Membership Store
nodes.description: Group -> member list, sharded by group id.

nodes.label: Push Notification Service
nodes.description: APNs/FCM bridge to wake offline devices.

nodes.label: Media Service + Blob Store
nodes.description: Stores image/video/voice attachments; messages carry a media id.

functional: Send a 1:1 message; deliver in real time if the recipient is online.
Buffer messages for offline recipients and deliver on reconnect.
Group messaging: a message goes to all current group members.
Sync message history across a user's multiple devices.
nonFunctional: Real-time delivery: online-to-online latency well under a second.
Reliable: an accepted message is never lost, even across crashes.
Ordered: messages within a conversation arrive in send order.
Scale to hundreds of millions of concurrent persistent connections.
Highly available; tolerate gateway and region failures.

capacity.label: Concurrent connections
capacity.note: Each user device holds one persistent socket.

capacity.label: Connections per gateway
capacity.note: Drives gateway node count and memory.

capacity.label: Avg message size
capacity.note: Media goes via blob store, not the message path.

api.description: Open a persistent WebSocket. The gateway registers the device in the session registry.
api.response: connection established; server may push queued messages

api.description: Client sends a message frame over its socket. Server persists, routes, and acks.
Post to a group; fanned out to all members' queues/gateways.

dataModel.name: messages
dataModel.note: Durable message log, sharded by conversation id; ordered by server sequence.

dataModel.name: delivery_state
dataModel.note: Per-recipient receipt state for a message.

dataModel.name: sessions
dataModel.note: Live routing table: which gateway holds each device's connection.

dataModel.name: group_members
dataModel.note: Group membership, sharded by group id.

patterns.name: Persistent connection (WebSocket)
patterns.what: Keep a long-lived socket per device so the server can push messages instantly.
patterns.whenToUse: Real-time delivery where polling latency/overhead is unacceptable.

patterns.name: Connection routing registry
patterns.what: A fast lookup of device -> gateway so any service can reach an online user's socket.
patterns.whenToUse: When millions of stateful connections are spread across many gateway nodes.
patterns.group: Route a Message to the Recipient's Connection

patterns.name: Offline message queue
patterns.what: Persist undelivered messages per recipient and drain on reconnect.
patterns.whenToUse: Recipients are frequently offline but must not miss messages.

patterns.name: Idempotent message id
patterns.what: Client-generated id dedups retries so a flaky network doesn't double-send.
patterns.whenToUse: At-least-once delivery over unreliable mobile networks.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-write (groups)
patterns.what: Expand a group message to each member at send time.
patterns.whenToUse: Group sizes are bounded; per-member delivery state is needed.

patterns.name: Per-conversation ordering
patterns.what: Assign a monotonic sequence per conversation so messages display in order.
patterns.whenToUse: Chat where out-of-order display is confusing.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Poll the Server for New Messages
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. The client sends a message with an ordinary HTTP POST; the server writes it to a messages table. To receive, each client polls the server every few seconds (GET /messages?since=...) and the server returns any new rows.

This is correct and trivial to build — and it exposes the two problems the rest of the design exists to solve. First, latency: a message sits unseen until the recipient's next poll, so 'real-time' is really 'every few seconds', and shortening the interval only makes it worse. Second, load: hundreds of millions of clients polling a database every few seconds is mostly empty responses — a crushing, wasteful read load whether or not anyone sent anything.

So the baseline motivates everything that follows: replace polling with a persistent connection the server can push down, so delivery is instant and the server only does work when there's an actual message.

view.caption: The client connects over WSS to the Connection Load Balancer while the Chat Service persists each message to the Message Store.

steps.decisionPrompt: If every client polls the server every 3 seconds for new messages, what does that cost at hundreds of millions of users — and how 'real-time' is it?
whyNow: Establishing the polling baseline first makes it obvious why a persistent connection is needed — it removes both the latency and the wasted-poll load in one move.

recap.after: Messages POSTed and stored; recipients poll the DB for new rows.
recap.newRisk: Polling is high-latency and floods the database with mostly-empty reads; the server can't push, so delivery is never truly real-time.

traps.trap: Shipping HTTP polling as 'good enough' for chat.
traps.why: Short poll intervals multiply database load across hundreds of millions of clients while still delivering messages seconds late.
traps.instead: Hold a persistent connection per device so the server pushes messages the instant they arrive.

steps.title: 2. Persistent Connections via WebSocket Gateways
description: Polling can't deliver in real time at this scale. Each device opens a long-lived WebSocket to a gateway node that holds the connection open and can push messages down instantly.
A gateway holds ~1M connections; a connection load balancer spreads new connections across the fleet. The connection is stateful, so it's sticky to one gateway for its lifetime.
Gateways are dumb pipes: they own the socket and (de)serialize frames, but delivery logic lives in the chat service behind them.

view.caption: The client connects over WSS to the Connection Load Balancer, which assigns it to a WebSocket Gateway that forwards sent messages to the Chat Service.

options.name: Persistent WebSocket (default)
options.description: Each device holds one long-lived full-duplex WebSocket to a sticky gateway node that keeps the socket open and pushes frames down the instant a message arrives.
pros: A single full-duplex connection lets the server push a message the instant it arrives — true real-time.
No repeated request overhead per poll, so it scales to hundreds of millions of idle connections cheaply.
Bidirectional: typing indicators, receipts, and presence flow both ways over one socket.
cons: Each gateway must hold millions of long-lived connections, with state to recover on node loss.
Mobile networks drop sockets, so clients need reconnect-and-resync logic.
Stateful connections complicate load balancing and rolling deploys.

view.caption: The client holds one long-lived WebSocket through the Connection Load Balancer to a sticky WebSocket Gateway, which relays messages to the Chat Service.

options.name: HTTP long-polling
options.description: The client issues an HTTP request the server holds open until a message arrives or it times out, then immediately re-polls.
pros: Works everywhere HTTP does — no special protocol, friendly to proxies and old clients.
Server holds the request open and replies when a message arrives, so it's near-real-time.
Simpler to reason about than persistent sockets; degrades to normal request/response.
cons: Each delivered message costs a full request/response cycle plus a re-poll, multiplying overhead.
Server-to-client push for the next message waits for the client to re-establish the poll.
Connection churn is high at scale, wasting capacity versus one stable socket.

view.caption: The Connection Load Balancer holds the client's HTTP request open at a Long-Poll Gateway until a message arrives, which then relays to the Chat Service.

options.name: Periodic polling
options.description: Clients ask the server 'anything new?' on a fixed timer, with no held connection or server push.
pros: Dead simple: clients ask 'anything new?' on a timer — trivial server logic.
Stateless on the server, so any node can answer any poll.
Easy to cache and rate-limit.
cons: Latency equals the poll interval, so 'real-time' is really 'every few seconds'.
Hundreds of millions of clients polling every few seconds is enormous wasted load when nothing changed.
No server push at all — strictly worse for a chat product.

view.caption: The client polls the Chat Service poll endpoint via the load balancer every 3 seconds, and the service persists messages to the Message Store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why a persistent WebSocket rather than HTTP long-polling or periodic polling?

concepts.definition: A node that terminates many long-lived bidirectional sockets and relays frames.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enables server push with low latency and no polling overhead.
concepts.example: One gateway holds 1M sockets; the cluster holds hundreds of millions.

whyNow: The connection model is the foundation — everything about routing, presence, and delivery depends on where the live socket lives.

recap.after: Devices hold live sockets to gateways; server can push.
recap.newRisk: Connections are spread across many gateways — how does a sender's message find the recipient's gateway?

traps.trap: Putting delivery logic in the gateway.
traps.why: Stateful gateways become hard to scale and deploy; a restart drops connections AND logic.
traps.instead: Keep gateways thin; route to a stateless chat service for delivery.

steps.title: 3. Route a Message to the Recipient's Connection
description: When a message arrives, the chat service looks up the recipient in the session registry: which gateway (if any) currently holds their device's socket.
If online, the chat service tells that gateway to push the frame down the recipient's socket. The registry is the live device -> gateway map, updated on connect/disconnect.
Keep the registry in a fast store (e.g. Redis) keyed by device id; it's rebuildable from gateways re-registering, so it isn't the durable source of truth for messages.

view.caption: The WebSocket Gateway hands a sent message to the Chat Service, which persists it to the Message Store, checks the Session / Presence Registry, and pushes to the recipient's online gateway.

options.name: Session registry lookup (default)
options.description: On a message, the chat service looks up the recipient's device-to-gateway mapping in a fast session registry and tells exactly those gateways to push the frame.
pros: A presence registry maps user/device to its current gateway, so the chat service routes only to the right gateways.
No wasted fanout — a message goes precisely to the devices that are online.
Handles three devices on three gateways by looking up all active sessions for the user.
cons: The registry is on the hot path and must be a fast, highly-available cache.
Registry staleness (just-disconnected device) causes a delivery miss that falls back to the offline path.
Every connect/disconnect writes to the registry, adding churn.

view.caption: The Chat Service persists to the Message Store, looks up the recipient's gateway in the Session / Presence Registry, and pushes the frame to exactly that WebSocket Gateway.

options.name: Pub/sub broadcast per user
options.description: Each user has a broker topic; gateways subscribe for their connected users and the chat service just publishes, letting the broker fan out to subscribed gateways.
pros: Each user has a topic; gateways subscribe for their connected users and the chat service just publishes.
No central registry lookup — the broker handles fanout to whichever gateways are subscribed.
Naturally delivers to multiple devices: all subscribed gateways receive the publish.
cons: Hundreds of millions of per-user topics strain the broker's subscription state.
Without per-user routing, a publish may touch gateways that no longer hold the user.
Broker becomes a critical, high-fanout dependency to scale and operate.

view.caption: The Chat Service publishes to the recipient's per-user topic on the Pub/Sub Broker, which delivers to whichever WebSocket Gateways subscribe for that user.

steps.decisionPrompt: A user has three devices on three different gateways. How does a message reach all of them?
whyNow: Routing across a sharded connection fleet is the core real-time problem; the session registry is how it's solved.

concepts.definition: A live mapping of device/user -> the gateway holding its connection.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets any backend node locate and push to an online device.
concepts.example: Lookup u_42's devices -> [gw7, gw19] -> push to both.
concepts.group: Route a Message to the Recipient's Connection

recap.before: Sockets exist but are unreachable from the backend.
recap.after: Any message can find an online recipient's gateway.
recap.newRisk: Recipient may be offline — the message still must not be lost.

steps.title: 4. Reliable, Ordered, Idempotent Delivery
description: Persist the message durably before acking the sender — that's what makes 'never lost' true across crashes. The store assigns a per-conversation sequence number for ordering.
The client attaches a clientMsgId; the server dedups on it so a retry over a flaky network doesn't create a duplicate (at-least-once + idempotency = effectively-once).
Track delivery state per recipient (sent -> delivered -> read); receipts flow back as small control messages and update the state.

view.caption: The WebSocket Gateway sends each message to the Chat Service, which persists it to the Message Store and pushes it to the recipient's online device for ordered, idempotent delivery.

steps.decisionPrompt: The client sends, the network drops the ack, the client retries. How do you avoid a duplicate message?

concepts.definition: A client-generated id the server uses to dedup retried sends.
concepts.whyItMatters: Mobile networks make retries common; without dedup, users see double messages.
concepts.example: Second send with the same clientMsgId returns the original serverMsgId.

concepts.definition: A monotonic counter per conversation defining message order.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees consistent ordering across devices regardless of arrival timing.
concepts.example: Devices sort by sequence, not by local receive time.

whyNow: Reliability, ordering, and dedup are the correctness heart of chat; getting them explicit is the senior signal.

recap.after: Durable, ordered, dedup'd messages with receipt state.
recap.newRisk: Recipient offline -> need a buffer; presence/receipts add traffic.

failureDrills.scenario: Chat service crashes after persisting but before acking.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Client retries with the same clientMsgId; server recognizes it and re-acks without duplicating.

flows.name: Persist-before-ack with idempotent dedup
flows.note: The message is durably stored and ordered before the sender is acked; a retry with the same clientMsgId returns the original id instead of duplicating.

steps.title: 5. Offline Delivery and History Sync
description: If the recipient is offline (no session in the registry), enqueue the message in their per-user message queue. On reconnect, the gateway drains the queue down the new socket before live traffic.
Wake the device with a push notification (APNs/FCM) so it reconnects and pulls. Push is a hint, not the delivery channel — the durable queue is.
History sync across devices reads from the durable message store by conversation sequence, so a new or returning device catches up to the same ordered view.

view.caption: The Chat Service checks the Session / Presence Registry, enqueues offline messages to the Per-User Message Queue (drained on reconnect), and wakes the device via the Push Notification Service.

options.name: Per-user message queue (push to inbox) (default)
options.description: An offline recipient's messages accumulate in their durable per-user queue, which the gateway drains down the new socket on reconnect.
pros: Each user has a durable queue; messages for an offline user accumulate and drain on reconnect.
Delivery is push-on-reconnect, so the client gets everything it missed in order.
Decouples senders from recipient availability — the sender's write always succeeds.
cons: Storing per-user queues for hundreds of millions of users is significant state.
A user offline for days on a new device needs a separate history-sync path beyond the queue.
Queue growth for very inactive users must be bounded or archived.

view.caption: For an offline recipient the Chat Service enqueues to the durable Per-User Message Queue (drained on reconnect) and wakes the device via the Push Notification Service.

options.name: Pull from message store on reconnect
options.description: Messages are persisted once in the message store; on reconnect the client pulls everything after its last-seen id rather than draining a dedicated queue.
pros: Messages are persisted once in the message store; on reconnect the client pulls everything after its last-seen id.
No per-user queue to maintain — the durable store is the single source of truth.
History sync to a new device is the same query with an earlier cursor.
cons: Reconnect triggers a potentially large catch-up read, spiking load after outages.
The client must track and send a reliable last-seen cursor per chat.
Waking an offline device still needs a separate push to trigger the pull.

view.caption: The Push Notification Service wakes the device; on reconnect the client pulls messages after its cursor as the Chat Service reads the Message Store from the last-seen id.

steps.decisionPrompt: Recipient is offline for two days, then reconnects on a new phone. What should they see?
whyNow: Offline is the common case on mobile; the durable per-user queue + push is the standard answer and ties back to 'never lost'.

recap.after: Offline users buffered + woken; history syncs across devices.
recap.newRisk: Group messages multiply this work per member — need fanout.

steps.title: 6. Group Messaging and Fanout
description: A group message must reach every current member. The fanout service reads the group membership, then for each member does the same online/offline decision: push to their gateway or enqueue.
Per-recipient delivery state means receipts work in groups too (delivered/read counts). Bound group size so fanout stays cheap; very large 'broadcast' groups may switch to a pull model.
Store one canonical message; fan out references plus per-member delivery rows — don't duplicate the body per member.

view.caption: The Chat Service hands group messages to the Group Fanout Service, which lists members from the Group Membership Store and enqueues a copy into each Per-User Message Queue drained on reconnect.

options.name: Fanout-on-write to per-member queues (default)
options.description: On send, the fanout service writes the message into each current member's queue, so online members get it immediately and offline members find it waiting.
pros: On send, the fanout service writes the message into each member's queue, so delivery to online members is immediate.
Read path is trivial — each member just drains their own queue.
Offline members already have the message waiting when they reconnect.
cons: A 256-member group means 256 queue writes per message — write amplification.
Very large groups make each send expensive and bursty.
Duplicates the message across many queues, increasing storage.

view.caption: On send, the Group Fanout Service lists members from the Group Membership Store and writes the message into each member's Per-User Message Queue.

options.name: Fanout-on-read (shared group log)
options.description: The message is appended once to a shared group log that each member reads from on demand via their own per-group read cursor.
pros: The message is written once to a shared group log; members read from it on demand.
No write amplification — one append regardless of group size, ideal for huge groups.
Storage is a single copy per message, not one per member.
cons: Each member must track a per-group read cursor and pull, adding read-path complexity.
Online members don't get an instant push unless you still notify them to read.
A burst of members reading a hot group log creates read fanout instead.

view.caption: The Chat Service lists members from the Group Membership Store and appends the message once to the Shared Group Log, which each member's WebSocket Gateway reads at its own cursor.

steps.decisionPrompt: A 256-member group gets a message; 100 members are offline. What work happens?
whyNow: Group fanout reuses the 1:1 delivery primitives and is the natural scaling step; bounding group size is the tradeoff to name.

recap.after: Group messages fan out to each member with per-member receipts.
recap.newRisk: Large groups and presence updates create fanout amplification; gateways are a scaling pressure point.

bottlenecks.issue: Huge group floods the fanout service.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Cap group size; for broadcast-scale, switch to pull/feed model.

bottlenecks.issue: Presence/typing updates amplify across large groups.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Throttle and batch presence; don't fan out typing to huge groups.

flows.name: Group message fanout to members
flows.note: One canonical message is fanned out to each member; per member the online/offline decision is made independently.

steps.title: 7. Presence, Media, and Gateway Resilience
description: Presence (online / last-seen) derives from session registry entries and heartbeats; broadcast presence changes only to a user's contacts, not everyone, and rate-limit typing indicators.
Media never travels the message path: the client uploads to the media/blob service and the message carries a media id; recipients download from the blob store/CDN.

When a gateway dies, its connections drop and clients reconnect (to a new gateway, re-registering in the session registry) and drain any queued messages — so a gateway loss is a brief reconnect, not data loss.

view.caption: The WebSocket Gateway registers connections in the Session / Presence Registry, while clients upload and download blobs to the Media Service + Blob Store and the Chat Service validates media ids against it.

steps.decisionPrompt: A whole gateway node crashes with 1M connections. What is the user-visible impact?
whyNow: Closing on presence/media/resilience maps the remaining requirements and shows the design degrades gracefully under failure.

concepts.definition: Liveness inferred from periodic pings on the live socket plus a TTL in the registry.
concepts.whyItMatters: Accurate online/last-seen without a separate presence write path.
concepts.example: Missed heartbeats past TTL -> mark offline -> notify contacts.

recap.after: Presence, media, and graceful gateway failover handled.
recap.newRisk: Reconnect storms after a gateway loss; presence fanout cost — both bounded by batching and backoff.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Those clients detect the drop and reconnect (with backoff) to other gateways, re-register, and drain queues.
failureDrills.mitigation: Stateless durable backend + rebuildable session registry + client reconnect with jittered backoff.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Real-Time Chat
finalDesign.description: Devices hold WebSocket connections to thin gateways; a session registry maps device -> gateway. The chat service persists each message (per-conversation sequence, dedup by clientMsgId) before acking, then routes: push to the recipient's gateway if online, else enqueue in their durable per-user queue and send a wake push. Groups fan out per member; receipts and presence flow as control messages; media goes via a separate blob/CDN path. Gateways are replaceable — clients reconnect and drain queues on failure.

view.caption: Clients connect over WSS through the Connection Load Balancer to WebSocket Gateways tracked in the Session / Presence Registry; the Chat Service persists to the Message Store, pushes to online devices or the Per-User Message Queue, fans out groups via the Group Fanout Service, wakes offline devices through push, and validates media against the blob store.

functional.how: Persistent socket + session-registry routing + gateway push.
Per-user durable queue drained on reconnect; push to wake.
Fanout service expands to members and reuses delivery primitives.
Per-recipient delivery state; presence from heartbeats.
Durable message store read by conversation sequence.

nonFunctional.how: Direct push over live sockets, no polling.
Persist before ack; durable offline queue.
Sharded gateway fleet + rebuildable session registry.
Thin stateless gateways; clients reconnect and drain.

say: Confirm real-time 1:1 + group, offline delivery, receipts, and history sync.
Pin sub-second delivery and 'never lost' as the hard guarantees.
Establish the scale: hundreds of millions of live connections.
Session registry to route to a recipient's gateway.
Delivery: ordering by sequence, dedup by clientMsgId, receipts.
Offline queue + push wake; history sync.
Group fanout; presence and media paths.
Tradeoffs: at-least-once+dedup vs exactly-once, group-size limits, presence cost.
Mention E2E encryption and multi-region as extensions.

expectations: Uses WebSockets and a session lookup.
May miss dedup/ordering and offline handling.
Designs persist-before-ack, idempotent send, and per-conversation ordering.
Reasons about gateway sharding, reconnect storms, and registry rebuildability.
Discusses presence fanout cost, multi-region routing, and E2E encryption tradeoffs.

followUps: How would you add end-to-end encryption without breaking server-side fanout/receipts?
How do you handle a reconnect storm when a whole region's gateways fail over?
How would you support very large broadcast groups (10K+ members)?
How do you keep presence accurate without flooding contacts with updates?
How would you order messages across multiple senders in a group fairly?

links.why: Production message-storage migration with Cassandra, ScyllaDB, partitions, and hot data concerns.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Cassandra: A Decentralized Structured Storage System
links.why: Useful for high-write distributed storage, eventual consistency, and wide-column message histories.
